english: blissful existence (hence: dé-riṅ (de ring) lhá-yi (lha yi) nyi-ma (nyi ma) šàr (shar) 'this day was a day of happiness to me' Glr., and similar expressions are of frq. occurrence; v. also lha-yúl (lha yul) and to be possessed of qualities and capacities superior to those of ordinary human beings. Nevertheless, when compared with any Buddhist saint, they are considered to be of inferior rank and power; and a local 'lha' (lha) can never attain to Buddhaship, without having previously assumed the nature of man; v. Köpp. I, 122. 248. II, 296. lhai (lha'i) bu (bu) or sras (sras), bú-mo (bu mo) or srás-mo (sras mo) descendant from the gods, son or daughter of the gods Dzl. and elsewh.; lha (lha) mi (mi) tób-pa (thob pa) to obtain the birth as a god or as man Thgy.; lhai (lha'i) yi-ge (yi ge) the Lantsa-letters, v. lan-tsa (lan tsa); lha (lha) ₒbáb-pa ('bab pa) the (mystical) entering of a deity into a human being, inspiration, so that the person inspired pronounces the oracles of the god, lha (lha) žúgs-pa (zhugs pa) id. pop.; also: the person thus inspired; lha (lha) ni (ni) kyab-júg-la (khyab 'jug la) mos (mos) as for gods, they worshipped Vishnu Pth., lha (lha) máₒmo-la (ma mo la) byed (byed) they worshipped the Mamo as a 'lha' (lha) Pth.; lhai (lha'i) dbáṅ-po (dbang po), lha-bdág (lha bdag), = brgya-byín (brgya byin) Indra; in another sense: lhai (lha'i) rgyál-po (rgyal po) byéd-pa (byed pa) to make the gods subject to one's self, (said to be the blessed consequence of a certain charm) Do.; the following gods are occasionally mentioned: nám-mkai (nam mkha'i) lha (lha) the deity of heaven Tar.; that of the sea Dzl.; the gods of trees, of houses etc.; dgrá-lha (dgra lha) and pó-lha (pho lha) are two personal gods of every human individual, the one being the god of the right side, the other of the left side of his body Glr., Mil.; yi-dam-lha (yi dam lha) v. yi-dam (yi dam). -- 2. the image of a god, in as much as it is really thought to be inhabited by a deity, after having been duly inaugurated (ráb-tu (rab tu) gnáṣ-pa (gnas pa)) Glr.; the local 'lha' (lha) are not always represented by figures resembling human beings, but even by sticks, stones and similar fetishes; gods also of non-Buddhist nations are called 'lha' (lha) and are acknowledged as such. -- 3. fig.: mii (mi'i) lha (lha) a king Mil., and hence 'lha' (lha) is often used in addressing a king, like the French Sire! lha-rgyal-yab-yúm (lha rgyal yab yum) the royal father and mother Pth.; lhá-yi (lha yi) lha (lha) the lord of lords, the supreme being, Buddha Cs. Comp. lha-káṅ (lha khang) an idol-shrine. -- lha-gčíg (lha gcig) 1. dear Sir! dear Lord and King! Pth. 2. princess Glr. -- lha-lčám (lha lcam) a princess Pth. -- lha-čéṅ (lha chen) a great and mighty deity. -- lhá-čos (lha chos) v. čos (chos) no. 2. -- lhá-rǰe (lha rje) physician Wdn. -- lha-rtén (lha rten) image or temple of a god or of the gods. -- lha-tó (lha tho) (prob. for lhai (lha'i) to-yór (tho yor)) heaps of stones, erected on mountain passes or on the tops of mountains. -- lha-ₒdre (lha 'dre) gods and goblins, lha (lha) klu (klu) ₒdre ('dre) sriṅ (srin) gdúg-pa (gdug pa) maṅ (mang) Glr. 2. goblin, hobgoblin; *lhá-ₒdre-ber-ka* (lha 'dre ber ka) mullein (Verbascum) Lh.; *lán-ṭe (lan Te) non (non) soṅ* (song) W. I have had the night-mare. -- lhá-sde (lha sde), mi-sde (mi sde) the class of the gods, the class of men. -- lha-nád (lha nad) Sch.: hysterics; madness. -- lha-pyág (lha phyag) honours shown to the 'lha' (lha), worship paid to them, pyogs (phyogs) bžiŕ (bzhir) lha-pyág (lha phyag) máṅ-po (mang po) btsál-te (btsal te) bowing towards the four points of the compass in token of reverence to the 'lha' (lha) Pth., often only: compliments, kind regards, offered to respected persons, in letters. -- lha-pyí (lha phyi) n. of a mountain in the south of Tibet Mil. -- lha-prúg (lha phrug) a descendant from the gods, child of the gods; as a mask Schl. 235. -- lha-prán (lha phran) inferior deities. -- lha-bris-mkán (lha bris mkhan) or -pa (pa) a painter of gods. -- lha-ₒbáṅs (lha 'bangs) Tar.; Schf.: slaves (doing service) in temples. -- lha-ma-yín (lha ma yin), lha-mín (lha min), असुर (asura), one of the six classes of beings, living on the slopes of the Sumeru below the 'lha' (lha) against whom, like Titans, they are continually fighting; also lha-ma-yín-mo (lha ma yin mo) are mentioned. -- lha-ma-srín (lha ma srin) gods, Asuras and Rakshasas, or perh. also: lha-ma-srín (lha ma srin) sde (sde) brgyad (brgyad) the eight classes of the gods, Asuras, Rākshasas etc., i.e. the whole world of spirits. -- lhá-mo (lha mo) goddess. -- lha-ma-rtá (lha ma rta) Sch.: a certain insect. -- lhá-bzo (lha bzo) 1. the art of making images of gods Pth. 2. also lha-bzó-bo (lha bzo bo) a framer of gods Glr. -- lhá-yul (lha yul) 1. the region of the world where the gods reside, the heaven, seat or abode of the gods. 2. fig.: a blessed country, a paradise. -- lhá-sa (lha sa) (in early times lha-ldán (lha ldan)) Sch., Köpp. II, 332. n. of the capital of Tibet. -- lha-srín (lha srin) gods and Rakshasas; steṅ (steng) lha- (lha-)
